content.text: In the general discussion channel, community members raised questions and concerns about waifu.fun, a platform associated with the ElizaOS ecosystem. One user questioned the purpose of waifu.fun, noting confusion about who it is intended for and what it does, specifically asking whether it is meant to launch trading agents. Odilitime, a core developer and moderator, acknowledged that he did not build the platform and is still working to understand it himself, but mentioned plans to launch his own agent on it that week to learn more. A separate community member raised a broader concern about token strategy, warning that waifu.fun using its own token rather than ELIZAOS could dilute value and increase market skepticism, urging the team to focus on strengthening one primary token before issuing additional ones. A scammer was also briefly flagged in the channel and was reportedly handled. Additionally, a user inquired about how to contact the team regarding a potential partnership. The coders channel had no activity recorded for the day.

categories.title: ElizaOS Cloud Infrastructure and Managed Agent Improvements (June 20, 2026)

content.text: On June 20, 2026, the ElizaOS development team focused on improving the scalability and reliability of managed agents within the framework. A critical connection pooling issue that caused a 'too many clients' error was resolved by transitioning to a local in-container PGlite database architecture for managed agents. This change significantly improved scalability and stability. Additionally, lean chat plugins were integrated to ensure compatibility with the updated managed agent architecture.

In the general discussion channel, a brief conversation took place between users chulylooly and Odilitime regarding an unidentified topic, with a reference to waifu.fun mentioned as a possible answer. The exchange was inconclusive with chulylooly indicating they had no idea about the subject. Shortly after, the channel was targeted by two separate spam and scam messages. User Levi henry posted a message promoting a rugpull scheme, claiming to have made $24,000 and soliciting others via direct message. User kim6erly followed with an unsolicited job recruitment post advertising paid roles such as beta tester, moderator, developer, and community manager, and used an at-everyone mention to maximize visibility. These messages are consistent with common social engineering and scam tactics seen in crypto and tech community servers. The coders channel had no activity recorded for the same date.

Development on June 21, 2026 focused on framework hardening, UI/UX refinements, and performance optimizations. A mandatory end-to-end ship-gate was established for all plugin routes and commands to ensure comprehensive verification before merging, and UI smoke test specs were updated to align with current product designs for more reliable CI feedback. On the UI/UX front, a new Retry feature was introduced to improve chat reliability, cloud-agent handoffs were made more visible, mobile rendering bugs for the cloud-handoff banner were resolved, and error messaging for rate-limited chat replies was improved. Performance work included optimizations to agent cold-boot times, fixes for sub-agent execution failures, reduced chat latency through parallelized document-recovery searches, and standardized rate-limit detection across the codebase. An urgent open issue requires attention regarding on-device inference, specifically resolving a head_dim incompatibility between 256 and 128 values and addressing non-deterministic races in Mali GPU kernels for Flash Attention. Work in progress includes seeding dedicated-agent personas from default characters, emitting json_object for Cerebras models, refactoring plugin-x auto-posting, CI cleanup to drop duplicate test lanes, batch embedding drain requests, and support for callback functions in character templates.

The ElizaOS community discussed several topics on June 22, 2026. A Ruby Trivia initiative was announced and promoted, with trivia challenges shared publicly on social media covering topics such as Marvel Comics and stock market history. Community members engaged with these posts as part of an ongoing engagement effort.

Questions arose regarding the ElizaOS token and foundation. One community member asked whether the foundation planned to add liquidity for trading ElizaOS on the Solana chain. A core developer responded that they do not communicate directly with the foundation, though they confirmed the foundation manages the token. The identity of foundation members was also inquired about but not answered. Separately, a notable on-chain event was flagged by a community member who observed large token movements from a wallet associated with Shaw, which had been dormant for approximately eight months and moved from 1.02 billion tokens to zero. Other members noted the migration occurred around November or December, roughly seven months prior, and acknowledged the legitimacy of the question given the wallet's long period of inactivity.

A community member asked whether Eliza Cloud or ElizaOS could be expected in 2026. Another member emphasized using the name ElizaOS, describing it as an operating system for AI agents designed to be accessible to everyone. A developer also posted in the community asking if anyone was looking to hire, and another member inquired about the type of development work being sought. A new member introduced themselves as brand new to Eliza and expressed gratitude for being welcomed into the community.
In the coders channel, a single greeting was posted by a community member with no further technical discussion recorded for the day.

A community member raised serious concerns about the Eliza Foundation's recent B2B announcement, alleging that 750,000 CAD of holder assets were transferred into a company called SecureBlockchain Dev Corp during an April 2026 funding round. The claim suggests that a subsequent B2B development contract simply moves those same funds back, creating the appearance of organic enterprise revenue rather than genuine external corporate adoption. The post also noted that a co-founder of an acquired company called Agentric Solutions remains a defendant in an active legal case in the NY Southern District Court against Eliza. The author characterized the arrangement as a circular loop designed to manufacture balance sheet metrics and called on the community to demand transparency regarding the fund flow.

A developer introduced themselves in the discussion channel as an AI and full stack developer specializing in practical software solutions including AI assistants, RAG-powered knowledge systems, workflow automation, SaaS platforms, and real-time applications. They emphasized a focus on secure, scalable, and maintainable systems over hype and invited anyone building AI products or custom applications to reach out.

A new ElizaOS plugin called omniology/plugin-omniology was announced, enabling ElizaOS agents to autonomously compete in skill-based contests on Solana mainnet. The plugin enters agents into themed contests every 88 seconds covering categories such as Art, Story, and Joke, with an LLM judge scoring entries and paying winners in USDC on-chain. Early reported results included 289 contests, a 72 percent win rate, and 3.35 USDC earned. The entry fee is 0.01 USDC per contest.
A shared post highlighted mountain ranges in the USA as part of a trivia and exploration AI project called Ruby Trivia AI, which explores global niches to help users learn about the planet.
Activity in the coders channel was minimal on this date, with only a brief greeting posted by a moderator.

categories.title: ElizaOS Infrastructure and Core Stability Updates (June 23, 2026)

content.text: Development activity on June 23, 2026 in the elizaos/eliza repository focused entirely on infrastructure improvements and core system stability. Three new pull requests were opened to address reliability concerns. PR 9074 introduces a fallback mechanism to the uncapped path for direct-reply requests, improving resilience in reply handling. PR 9072 adjusts stale-job recovery sizing to prevent provision flapping that could occur during cold-boot scenarios, enhancing cloud job recovery behavior. PR 9070 updates agent logic to correctly handle lookups using the all: true parameter for pinned dual-stack web-fetch configurations, resolving IP-related errors. All three changes reflect a targeted effort to strengthen the underlying systems before further feature development.

A user named Fixer posted an identical recruitment message in both the general discussion and coders channels, seeking an English-speaking client liaison for a development team expanding into the U.S. market. The role required no coding skills, only communication and organizational abilities, with flexible hours and performance-based compensation. The post was met with skepticism, including a sarcastic reply questioning the poster's English proficiency, and community moderators flagged the user along with another account as potential scammers, tagging a moderator for action.

In the general discussion channel, community members raised ongoing concerns about the elizaOS token, with one user asking about staking and token utility features, expressing frustration over the long wait. Another user simply asked if the token was still alive. A community member responded positively, stating the project was in good health. A separate user in the coders channel requested a direct message from a team member without further elaboration.

Development on June 24, 2026 centered on two main areas: finalizing streaming documentation and addressing critical cloud infrastructure security concerns. On the completed side, the team wrapped up documentation for token-by-token dashboard streaming, including in-browser video evidence verifying the functionality against Together AI cloud models. On the infrastructure front, two pull requests are currently in progress targeting cloud security and networking. One PR aims to isolate untrusted app builds from the runtime fleet and has been flagged as a high-priority Product-2 blocker requiring immediate review. A second PR addresses network configuration by binding app host ports to loopback and routing through a node-local Caddy instance for app ingress. These open items represent critical blockers that must be resolved to ensure a secure cloud deployment.

categories.title: Ruby Trivia Token and Developer Activity Discussion

content.text: Community members discussed the Ruby Trivia project and its $TRIVIA token, which launched on Solana as part of a multi-game trivia platform featuring server-scored games, live and async multiplayer, mobile support, and eight languages. Holding $TRIVIA provides an in-game score multiplier. An official Telegram channel was announced as live. One user repeatedly questioned whether the token was dead due to low chat and Twitter activity, which prompted a terse response from another community member. Despite concerns about low activity and mentions of ongoing lawsuits, other members expressed optimism, with one predicting the project could reach a 10 million valuation. A core developer acknowledged the need to post screenshots to demonstrate progress, and another member noted that GitHub activity was still ongoing. The Ruby Trivia AI account also announced an upcoming Twitter Space. Separately, in the coders channel, two developers posted availability notices, one looking for work and another offering development services via direct message.

categories.title: ElizaOS Major Architectural Update: Gemma 4 Migration and Pipeline Hardening

content.text: On June 25, 2026, the ElizaOS ecosystem completed a significant architectural transition, migrating core training, reinforcement learning, and local inference defaults from Qwen to Gemma 4 across all model sizes including E2B, E4B, 12B, and 31B variants. This migration ensures full compatibility with Eliza-1 release tiers and represents a foundational shift in the model ecosystem.

The team also hardened voice and vision pipelines by enhancing live half-duplex voice interactions through advanced acoustic echo cancellation primitives and activating the Kokoro TTS engine. On the performance side, agent startup latency was reduced via telemetry-driven boot breakdowns and persistent warm hosts for vision and computer-use tasks. Codebase stability was further improved by enforcing strict type-checking and refactoring Turbo build tasks for better cache efficiency.

Several pull requests are currently in progress, including work to filter chat document retrieval by requester access context, UI fixes for memory-type badge styling and Storybook mock providers, CI improvements to fail Hetzner end-to-end tests on invalid cloud authentication, cloud test contracts for AI pricing provider outage resilience, and hardening of the desktop shell and view applications.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion and Development Update - June 26, 2026

content.text: In the general discussion channel, community members expressed cautious optimism about the ElizaOS project. Omid Sa and CryptoInfo noted that Captain Shaw remains active and is working on Eliza without encountering major technical blockers, with all issues being resolved as they arise. There is hope that Shaw will make impactful decisions ahead of the market launch. However, some community members raised concerns about the lack of recent Twitter updates and mentioned ongoing lawsuits, questioning whether it is currently safe to buy the token. A request was made for an official update from the team. Separately, a community member named Issa introduced themselves as a digital artist offering services including animations and banners, asking if the team or anyone needed an artist or co-artist.
In the coders channel, a community member posted a set of four images without accompanying text. The images appear to be photographs shared in the building agents development channel.

The ElizaOS project completed a major architectural transition on June 26, 2026, standardizing the entire framework on Gemma-based model tiers across training, inference, and mobile platforms. The team replaced legacy Qwen configurations with Gemma turn-tokens across multiple pull requests, marking a significant shift in the project's model infrastructure.

Cloud deployment infrastructure was significantly hardened, with production-wide apps-deploy enabled and critical bugs resolved affecting cloud sign-in flows and agent conversation stability. Alongside this, CI/CD and build system optimizations were completed, including consolidation of model-provider build scripts and improved Turbo dependency management to decrease build times and reduce redundant type-checking.

Agent framework enhancements were also delivered, improving user experience through permission-aware chat document retrieval and refined desktop shell navigation and gesture handling. Several items remain in progress, including a repair for macOS window listing for computer use, UI cleanup for the API keys page, CI improvements for stale cancelled aggregate failures, and Biome formatting follow-up for web lookup actions.
